artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down with a decaf after getting Ellie to bed and wiping pumpkin batter off literally every surface in this condo. You’d laugh – I had her “helping” with mini loaves this afternoon while Mark debugged something for work at the dining table.

I’ve been thinking about you a lot because so many little things at the café trace back to you and Dad. Your banana bread recipe is still our top seller, and now it has a cousin: the low‑glycemic line I started after Dad’s type 2 diabetes diagnosis back in May 2021. He came up from Red Deer again last weekend for our monthly taste‑test ritual. We tried v3 of the almond‑flour banana bread and a stevia‑sweetened pumpkin loaf I’ve been tweaking since October 2023. I brought my little notebook to the shop and we actually wrote down his blood sugar before and two hours after. His numbers are finally staying in the 7s instead of spiking into the 9s, and he said, “It still feels like a treat,” which made my whole week.

Sundays are my favourite now: our river valley walk with reusable cups, playground stop for Ellie, then farmers’ market. I keep reminding myself of my “no laptop Sundays” rule, even when I’m tempted to sneak a peek at the roasting schedule. Between that, the Wednesday night laundry call with you and Dad, and protecting one quiet evening a week for a novel and herbal tea, I’m trying hard not to burn out.

Business‑wise, Riverside is holding steady in that $100–500k turnover band, but cash flow still feels like juggling. I’m working with the accounting firm to figure out if a second roaster or a delivery van makes more sense for next year. Some days it feels like we’re a “real” company (incorporated, five full‑timers, seven part‑timers, office coffee contracts and all), and other days I’m just your kid in the kitchen, covered in flour.

Love you,
Amelia

metadata.subject: Dad’s diabetes bakes + trying not to burn out

artifacts.content:
Hey Liv,

Catching you between shifts, hopefully not on one of those brutal Calgary overnights. I was thinking about you a lot this week because Riverside has accidentally become this little hub for people trying to keep their heads above water.

Remember Priya from Ellie’s preschool, the ER nurse you sometimes pick up extra shifts with in Calgary? Back in January 2023 she and Jenna pulled me aside at Little Sprouts and told me a few families were quietly leaning on the school snack program because groceries in Edmonton had gotten so insane, especially after Jenna’s husband got laid off from Suncor. Since March 2023 I’ve been doing this low‑key “pay‑what‑you‑can” window at the 109 Street café on Wednesdays and Fridays from 3–5 p.m. for the preschool parents – brewed coffee and day‑old pastries. No social posts, no signage, just word of mouth in the hallway. It’s technically just clearing waste, but it feels like one small thing I can control.

On the other end of the spectrum, we’ve got Bright Path Counselling Collective on 109 Street as a wholesale client now – about $220 in beans every two weeks since March 2022. One of their therapists told me they see a lot of ER staff, including your colleague Liam O’Rourke after that horrible Foothills Medical Centre incident in October 2021. They like that our coffee is mellow and not super jittery; apparently it matters in trauma sessions.

It’s strange how a little roastery ends up tied into all these hard stories: layoffs, postpartum depression, PTSD, addiction. I’ve got Kyle, the Jasper Ave accountant whose addictions counselor at the Edmonton Mental Health Clinic on 97 Street told him in January to swap his nightly whiskey for a coffee ritual. He spends $45–$60 every two weeks on beans and basically lives at our Saturday brewing workshops to stay busy during his 12‑week outpatient program.

Anyway, enough heaviness. When are you actually coming up? I want to show you the new roaster setup and make you pull a shot that would make your barista sister proud.

Love,
A

metadata.subject: How the café turned into a tiny support network

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – Riverside Roasters + life balance

1. Core identity check
- I keep saying we’re “a small-batch specialty coffee roastery and café focused on ethically sourced beans and approachable coffee education for everyday people, not just coffee geeks.” Still true. Need to protect that as we grow.
- We’re both online and offline now: café on 111 Street, web store at https://www.riversideroasters.ca, subscriptions shipping across Alberta.

2. Quiet community things (do NOT market)
- Pay-what-you-can brewed coffee + day-old pastries: started March 2023 after talking with Jenna and Priya in January about Little Sprouts parents relying on the snack program. Keep it Wed/Fri 3–5 p.m., low-key, no social posts. Remember: Jenna’s husband laid off from Suncor; Priya juggling ER shifts in Calgary with Liv.
- YWCA domestic violence shelter workshops: since Oct 2022, after-hours at the 100 Street café. Sophie organizes the small group of women from the shelter. I cover the ~$45 per-person supply cost myself so it never hits their programming budget. Safety first: doors locked, no photos, no tags.
- Sunday evening “Coffee Skills for Newcomers” since Feb 2024 for Mom’s three Ukrainian families from her Red Deer church ESL group. Free. Practice café vocabulary + basic barista tasks so they can apply at Safeway on Jasper Ave, Tim Hortons by the High Level Bridge, etc. Keep handwritten list of full names, phone numbers, where they’ve applied behind the bar – update weekly.

3. Boundaries
- No more than one after-hours commitment per week (YWCA or Newcomers, not both).
- Protect Sunday daytime as family/no-laptop: river valley walk, playground with Ellie, farmers’ market.
- One evening for myself: novel + herbal tea, in bed by 10.

4. Money
- Gross yearly turnover: still in $100k–$500k band. Incorporated, not a side hustle. Need to review with accountant: second roaster vs. used delivery van.

Remember: I started this to build community and a sustainable life, not to run myself into the ground.

Event: After-hours barista skills + budgeting workshop – YWCA 100 Street

Date: 2024-05-16 (Thursday)
Time: 6:00 p.m. – 8:15 p.m.
Location: Riverside Roasters Coffee Co. – 100 Street café (closed to public)
Attendees: Amelia; Sophie Ellison; 6–8 women from the YWCA domestic violence shelter on 100 Street

Agenda / notes:
- 5:30 p.m.: Arrive early, pull shots, set up training station (two group heads, milk jugs, cleaning gear). Put “Closed for Private Training” sign on door.
- 6:00–6:20 p.m.: Welcome + quick check-in. Remind everyone: no photos, no tagging locations for safety. Sophie to handle introductions using first names only.
- 6:20–7:10 p.m.: Basic espresso skills: dialing in grind, pulling consistent shots, steaming milk safely. Focus on skills that translate to entry-level café jobs.
- 7:10–7:40 p.m.: Simple budgeting exercise: walk through a sample paycheque from a part-time barista job, rent/groceries/transport, and how tip pooling usually works.
- 7:40–8:00 p.m.: Q&A + practice time.
- 8:00–8:15 p.m.: Clean-up, debrief with Sophie.

Logistics / financials:
- Supplies: beans, milk (incl. oat/almond), pastries, printed handouts. Estimated supply cost ~$45 per person.
- I will personally absorb the supply cost again; do NOT invoice YWCA or add to Sophie’s programming budget line.
- Mark POS items as “training/waste” so it doesn’t show as a donation tied to specific names.

Reminders:
- Check that back door is locked; keep front door monitored.
- Have taxi/Uber numbers handy in case anyone needs a safe ride.
- Quick text to Mark around 5:30 to confirm Ellie bedtime coverage.
- Block off Friday morning roasting schedule so I’m not exhausted on the floor after a late night.

Hey Mark,

Just wanted to brain-dump a bit before I crash so I don’t end up waking you up at 1 a.m. with café talk again.

Today was a textbook “this is what Riverside is for” day. Morning rush was the usual – commuters, a couple of our office clients grabbing beans for their break rooms – but the part that stuck with me was all the quiet support stuff threaded through it.

First, Alex swung by on his lunch break. I slipped him a bag of the Guatemala single-origin and two of the CBD brownies I baked last night. Same routine as always since July 2022: first Friday of the month, keep it low-key, ring everything through as waste so there’s no paper trail with his name in case your company ever audits receipts. He looked tired but said the panic attacks after that March 2022 on-call incident aren’t as constant now, which is something.

Then in the afternoon, the Little Sprouts crew came through for the pay-what-you-can window. Jenna grabbed a coffee and muffin and just needed to vent about her husband still being out of work after the Suncor layoff. Priya popped in between ER shifts in Calgary and joked that she basically lives on our day-old banana bread. I’m glad the Wednesday/Friday 3–5 p.m. thing at the 109 Street café is becoming a predictable anchor for them.

And tonight I had Kyle in the brewing workshop again – the accountant whose addictions counselor at the Edmonton Mental Health Clinic on 97 Street told him to swap his nightly whiskey for a coffee ritual. He’s halfway through his 12-week outpatient program and proudly told the group he hasn’t had a drink in 26 days. He spent another $50 on beans and asked if we’d ever consider a later Saturday slot so people in recovery groups could still make it.

Sometimes I worry I’m taking on too much emotional weight, but then I look around and see all these little constellations of people holding each other up over cups of coffee, and it feels worth the 5:30 a.m. alarms.

Thanks for holding things down with Ellie when I have these long days.

Love,
A

metadata.subject: Today at the café felt like a whole microcosm

artifacts.content:
Event: Sunday “Coffee Skills for Newcomers” – Margaret’s ESL families

Date: 2024-04-21 (recurring every Sunday)
Time: 6:00 p.m. – 8:00 p.m.
Location: Riverside Roasters – 111 Street café (closed session)
Attendees: Amelia; 3 Ukrainian families from Mom’s Red Deer church ESL group

Description:
Weekly free training session I started in February 2024 for the three Ukrainian families Mom (Margaret) tutors in English through her church in Red Deer. Focus is on:
- Basic café / barista vocabulary (espresso, shot, grind, till, shift, closing duties, etc.).
- Simple barista tasks: wiping down bar, loading dishwasher, grinding + dosing, steaming milk safely.
- Practicing job interview questions and small talk in English.

Goal: help them feel confident applying for entry-level jobs at places like Safeway on Jasper Ave and the Tim Hortons by the High Level Bridge.

Logistics:
- Keep a handwritten list behind the bar with their full names, phone numbers, and where they’ve already applied. Update each week with any new applications or call-backs.
- No charge for the session; treat it as community time, not a “workshop product.”
- Lock front door at 6:05 p.m. and put up “Closed for Private Training” sign.
- Offer simple snacks (leftover pastries) and brewed coffee/tea.

Personal reminders:
- Ask Mom on Wednesday laundry call if there are any new families needing to join.
- Don’t schedule YWCA after-hours sessions on the same weekend; max one evening community commitment.
- Protect Sunday daytime as family/no-laptop (river valley walk, playground with Ellie, farmers’ market) so the evening doesn’t feel like I’ve worked all day.
- Bring printed job postings from Safeway/Tims bulletin boards to discuss.

Note: This is one of those “quiet good” things – no social media, no photos, just real practice and support.

metadata.title: Coffee Skills for Newcomers – weekly session

artifacts.content:
Hi Dad,

Writing this from the little office at the back of the 111 Street café while the roaster cools down. I’m still smiling about your visit last weekend.

Thank you for being my unofficial R&D department since your type 2 diabetes diagnosis back in May 2021. Those monthly drives up from Red Deer have turned into such a ritual. Testing the low-glycemic, no-added-sugar bakes with you – the almond-flour banana bread, the stevia pumpkin loaf we started trialing in October 2023 – has been way more fun (and more scientific!) than doing it alone. I’ve got your readings from this visit written in my notebook: fasting 6.9, 2-hour post-snack 7.3. Not bad at all.

I’ve been thinking about how many different kinds of people our coffee touches. There’s Christine Miller, the Grade 4 teacher from Glenora Elementary who used to come in every Thursday morning for drip + muffin before school. After her multiple sclerosis flare-up in November 2022 put her on short-term disability, she switched to a 1 kg medium roast subscription – $34 delivered to her condo near 104 Avenue every month. She emailed last week to say the delivery ritual makes her feel less cut off from the world.

Then there’s the LGBTQ+ support circle that meets here the first Thursday of every month at 7 p.m., organized by Mark’s friend Daniel Reyes from the Pride Centre of Edmonton. About 10–15 people, most not out at work or to their families yet. They each spend maybe $6–$10, but what they really seem to value is the Pride flag in the window and the little sign about our women-led Guatemalan cooperative. I top up their water and bus dishes and try to stay out of the way unless someone wants to talk about coffee.

Some days I wish you could just see the café during one of those evenings – the way people relax into the space. It makes all the loan repayments and 5:30 a.m. alarms feel worth it.

Love,
Amelia

metadata.subject: Your unofficial R&D + all the people our coffee reaches
